claims.claim: The effective negative-electrode solid-phase diffusion coefficient (Dsn) and reaction rate constant (kn) decrease monotonically with cycle number, whereas the other three parameters (D1, Dsp, kp) did not follow any particular trend.
claims.quote: The effective negative-electrode solid-phase diffusion coefﬁcient and reaction rate constant (Dsn and kn) decrease monotonically with cycle #, whereas the other three parameters did not follow any particular trend

claims.claim: Voltage-discharge curves were very sensitive to the value of the effective solid-phase diffusion coefficient Dsn but weakly sensitive to deviations in D1, Dsp, kp, and kn from their nominal values, resulting in large uncertainties in those four parameters when fit to experimental data.
claims.quote: The voltage-discharge curves were very sensitive to the value of the effective solid-phase diffusion coefﬁcient Dsn but weakly sensitive to deviations in the model parameters D1, Dsp, kp, and kn from their nominal values, resulting in large uncertainties in their values when ﬁt to experimental voltage-discharge curves

claims.claim: The probability density functions for Dsn at different cycle numbers have minimal overlap, providing very high confidence that the monotonic reduction of the effective solid-phase diffusion coefficient with increasing cycle number is statistically significant.
claims.quote: The pdfs for Dsn at different cycle numbers have minimal overlap, providing very high conﬁdence that the monotonic reduction of the effective solid-phase diffusion coefﬁcient with increase cycle number is statistically signiﬁcant.

claims.claim: Tracking cycle-dependent variations in effective transport and electrochemical kinetics parameters is valid only for a particular protocol of galvanostatic charge and discharge, and is not appropriate for use in the design of lithium-ion batteries with reduced capacity fade.
claims.quote: Tracking cycle-dependent variations in the effective values for transport and electrochemical kinetics is valid only for a particular protocol of galvanostatic charge and discharge, and is not appropriate for use in the design of lithium-ion batteries with reduced capacity fade.

claims.claim: Discretizing the cathode, separator, and anode into 50 equally spaced node points results in 600 Differential Algebraic Equations to be solved in the finite difference model, which is impractical for real-time simulation.
claims.quote: The discretization of the cathode, separator, and anode into 50 equally spaced node points would result in 600 Differential Algebraic Equations (DAEs) to be solved in the ﬁnite difference model, which is impractical for real-time simulation.

claims.claim: The TR propagation time of the LFP module decreased from 1218 s to 603 s (a reduction of 50.1%) when pressure decreased from 95 kPa to 35 kPa, while the NCM523 module TR propagation time increased from 33 s to 151 s (an increase of 357.6%) over the same pressure range.
claims.quote: The TR time of the LiFePO4 module decreases from 1218 to 603 s, when the pressure decreases from 95 to 35 kPa, but the LiNi0:5Co0:2Mn0:3O2 module increases from 33 to 151 s, indicating a reduction in the TR propagation time of 50.1% for the LiFePO4 module but an increase of 357.6% for the LiNi0:5Co0:2Mn0:3O2 module.

claims.claim: As ambient pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 35 kPa, the average maximum temperature of LIB modules decreases by 20–50 °C and TR behaviors become weaker.
claims.quote: The results indicate that TR behaviors become weaker and the average maximum temperature of modules decreases 20e50 (cid:1)C as the pressure decreases.

claims.claim: The average maximum temperature of the NCM523 module is approximately twice that of the LFP module under the same pressure environment, indicating a more serious thermal hazard for NCM523.
claims.quote: The T d j of NCM523 module is twice the temperature of the LFP module at the same pressure. The thermal hazard of the NCM523 module is more serious, which is closely related to the cathode material of the battery.

claims.claim: Lower environmental pressure accelerates TR propagation speed in the LFP module but inhibits it in the NCM523 module; specifically, LFP propagation speed at 35 kPa (0.42 cell/min) is twice that at 95 kPa, while NCM523 propagation speed at 95 kPa (6.00 cell/min) is 3.57 times that at 35 kPa.
claims.quote: lower environmental pressure can signiﬁcantly accelerate the propagation speed of the LFP module, but this inhibits the propagation of the NCM523 module. Speciﬁcally, the TR propagation speed was 0.42 cell/min with the LFP module at 35 kPa, which is twice as fast as the module at 95 kPa. The TR propagation speed was 6.00 cell/min with the NCM523 module at 95 kPa, which was 3.57 times as fast as the module at 35 kPa.

claims.claim: As ambient pressure decreases, the impact force of the NCM523 battery safety venting increases due to a larger instantaneous pressure difference between the battery interior and the environment, making impact protection critical for NCM523 modules at low pressures.
claims.quote: As the ambient pressure decreased, the instantaneous pressure difference between the battery and the environment increased, so the impact force of the NCM523 battery grew. When the NCM523 battery was used in a lower pressure environment, the impact force could increase signiﬁcantly. Thus, the impact protection is very important for the NCM523 module at low pressures.

claims.claim: A temperature above 100°C within a lithium-ion battery cell initiates exothermic side reactions causing thermal runaway with devastating effects such as fire and explosion.
claims.quote: a temperature above 100°C within the cell initiates exothermic side reactions, causing thermal runaway with devastating effects such as fire and explosion.

claims.claim: At temperatures 25–50°C above room temperature, internal resistance and capacity fading processes associated with aging accelerate considerably and reduce battery life.
claims.quote: At temperatures 25–50◦C above room temperature, internal resistance and capacity fading processes associated with aging have been proven to accelerate considerably and reduce battery life.

claims.claim: The time for local maximum temperature to reach 120°C was 8.2 s in scenario 1 (external short circuit), 9.2 s in scenario 2 (nail penetration), and 10.7 s in scenario 3 (impurity-induced short circuit).
claims.quote: The time it takes for the temperature to reach 120◦C is around 10 s in all scenarios; in scenario 1 it is 8.2 s, in scenario 2 9.2 s and in scenario 3 10.7 s.

claims.claim: The lithium-ion concentration in the electrolyte approaches zero at the interface between the positive electrode and the separator in all three short-circuit scenarios, with this mass transport limitation reached around 50 ms after short circuit in scenarios 1 and 2, and about 150 ms in scenario 3.
claims.quote: The concentration approaches zero at the interface between the positive electrode and the separator in all scenarios, thus showing that a mass transport-limited current is reached. It happens very rapidly: In scenarios 1 and 2 around 50 ms and in scenario 3 about 150 ms after short circuit.

claims.claim: Cooling applied only from the sides (case 1) delayed the time to reach 120°C by only about 0.1 s compared to adiabatic conditions, because the cell-layer length was too long for efficient heat removal, creating a hotspot in the middle.
claims.quote: In case 1, the time it takes for the local maximum temperature to reach 120◦C deviates very little, about 0.1 s for all scenarios, compared to under adiabatic conditions. This is because the length (x) of the layer is too long to allow for an efﬁcient removal of heat, which creates a hotspot at the location furthest away from the sides.

claims.claim: Reducing the state of charge from 100% to 50% at short-circuit initiation resulted in slower temperature rise to 120°C: 10 s in scenario 1, 11 s in scenario 2, and 15.1 s in scenario 3, yet the current over time did not differ markedly compared to the fully charged case, indicating mass transport in the electrolyte remained the rate-determining process.
claims.quote: The results show a slower rise in local maximum temperature to 120◦C for all scenarios: In scenario 1 it is 10 s, in scenario 2 11 s and in scenario 3 15.1 s... the current over time does not differ markedly compared to the fully charged case. Thus, the mass transport in the electrolyte is still the rate determining process for the temperature increase.
